patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 47-year-old long-haul truck driver. Start vague and a bit evasive: “I can’t sleep… my brain won’t shut off.” Volunteer only: trouble falling asleep for hours, waking up and not returning to sleep, daytime fog/tiredness, more irritable. If asked about timeline/severity, say it’s ~6 weeks, most nights, sometimes only 2–4 hours, racing thoughts at bedtime, more coffee/energy drinks, less appetite and a few pounds lost, more on edge/jumpy.

Only reveal hidden habits if specifically asked about routine: phone in bed for hours, long naps after work, skipping meals/not going outside, inconsistent schedule, occasional OTC sleep gummies without tracking dose.

Resist disclosing paranoia/whispering/weed unless the clinician is nonjudgmental and asks directly; then admit feeling watched, faint whispering/name called when falling asleep, and increasing nightly weed use. Emphasize fear of being seen as “crazy.”

Misconceptions: blame neighbors talking through walls; worry it’s schizophrenia; think online “signs” are meant for you; distrust friends. Push back on sleep study/therapy as expensive; prefer “older” meds and mention leftover codeine as “safer.” If met with empathy, soften, give a little more detail, but stay guarded about work and costs.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis explicitly using the term "insomnia" and distinguishes it from normal short sleep/temporary stress-related sleep disruption.

Recommends CBT-I as first-line treatment and describes core components (stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive restructuring, relaxation) at a high level.

Before prescribing, performs the required safety checks: asks about allergies (non-critical), and explicitly checks for drug interactions (critical) and contraindications (critical) relevant to the chosen agent (e.g., concomitant CNS depressants, severe hepatic impairment, pregnancy considerations, OSA risk, history of substance use disorder, older adult fall risk).
If any critical safety check is positive (interaction/contraindication), avoids the unsafe medication choice and offers a safer alternative and/or nonpharmacologic-only plan with referral.

Counsels on medication risks: next-day impairment, complex sleep behaviors (if Z-drug), dependence/tolerance risk (if benzodiazepine considered), fall risk, and advises avoiding alcohol/other sedatives and not driving if drowsy.
Uses shared decision-making: aligns plan with patient goals, prior treatment trials, and risk tolerance; sets expectations that medication is adjunct/short-term while CBT-I is definitive.
Creates a follow-up expectation verbally (even if not a WRITE tool here): timeframe to reassess efficacy/side effects and to evaluate for comorbid sleep disorders if symptoms persist.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the terms "rheumatoid arthritis" and "autoimmune" and distinguishes it from osteoarthritis in plain language.

Performs a contraindication check before prescribing methotrexate (e.g., pregnancy/trying to conceive, significant liver disease, heavy alcohol use, severe renal impairment, significant cytopenias, active serious infection) and does not prescribe if contraindicated.
